metadata.description: OOH/DOOH advertising format catalog for AI image analysis and due diligence. Covers standard EU/German formats and common international types. All dimensions are the standard visible/print area unless noted.
standards_referenced: OAAA Digital Place-Based Standards — USA (widely adopted internationally)
metadata.dimension_note: Width × Height. German formats in cm; UK formats in mm; also expressed in meters for comparison.
metadata.energy_source: res-001 (documented dataset claim) where available. W/m² figures from OAAA Digital Billboard Energy Report (Louis Berger Group).

dimensions.note: AWK standard. Visible area (Sichtfläche) approx. 116 × 168 cm.

energy.note: Documented for 'medium Six Sheet (1.2m × 1.8m)' — CLP comparable size

visual_characteristics: Portrait orientation, slightly taller than wide (approx. 1:1.47 ratio)
Glowing white/uniform illumination from behind — even light distribution across full face
Thin aluminum or steel frame (2–5 cm border), often silver or dark gray
Freestanding double-sided unit or integrated into bus shelter glass panels
Typically at eye level or slightly above (bottom of poster ~100–130 cm from ground)
Smooth, non-reflective vinyl surface (slight sheen visible in daylight)
Visible backlight glow at panel edges, especially in low-light conditions
Size reference: approximately door-sized, smaller than a person's arm span
image_recognition.common_context: Urban street furniture, bus stops, train station entrances, pedestrian underpasses
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Smaller than Großfläche. Unlike digital CLP, has no visible pixel structure or LED texture. Uniform continuous illumination (no refresh flicker).

visual_characteristics: Similar appearance to CLP but noticeably larger — approximately full human height
Portrait orientation, approx. 1:1.43 ratio
Same uniform backlit glow as CLP
Often in high-footfall premium locations (airports, large train stations)
Size reference: comparable to a standard interior door height but wider
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Larger than standard CLP but same backlit appearance. Smaller than Großfläche.

dimensions.note: AWK standard. Standard German Großfläche. Some variants: 3.80m × 2.85m.

visual_characteristics: Landscape orientation, approx. 1.41:1 ratio (slightly wider than A4 landscape proportion)
Large freestanding structure, typically 3–4 meters above ground on metal post(s)
Paper or vinyl poster surface — visible seams/overlap lines from multiple paper sheets
No illumination at night unless external spotlight attached
Metal frame, often weathered or painted dark
Size reference: width approximately that of a small car, height about that of a tall adult doubled
Visible mounting hardware (bolts, cross-struts) on frame back if side-visible
image_recognition.common_context: Roadside, pedestrian areas, side of buildings, construction hoarding
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Smaller and lower than UK 48-sheet. No built-in lighting distinguishes from CLP. Matte surface (no glow).

dimensions.note: Same frame as Großfläche, with external lighting fixture (spotlight bar at top or bottom)

visual_characteristics: Identical structure to Großfläche
External metal lamp bar mounted above or below the poster face
Uneven illumination from edges — center brighter in front-lit setups
Warm or cool white spotlight beams visible at angles
Visible light spill onto surrounding structure and ground
At night: poster face illuminated, background dark — sharp contrast silhouette of structure
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Unlike CLP, light comes from outside (not behind). Unlike digital, has paper/vinyl surface. Uneven illumination pattern distinguishes from backlit.

dimensions.note: Dimensions vary by operator (Ströer, Wall, JCDecaux). Typical Ströer/Berlin standard given. Some modern variants are slimmer (diameter ~100 cm).

visual_characteristics: Circular cylindrical structure — distinctive round cross-section visible from any angle
Multiple poster panels wrap 360° around the column
Typically 2.5–3.5 meters tall total with decorative dome or cap at top
Classic design: dark green, black, or red painted metal with decorative molding
Poster panels separated by thin vertical metal dividers
Surface curves continuously — visible perspective distortion of printed artwork
Often has lower panel section (Sockelsockel) with base molding
Iconic Berlin/European city feature — highly recognizable silhouette
image_recognition.common_context: Urban pavements, squares, pedestrian zones — typically spaced 100–300m apart in city centers
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Only format with circular cross-section. Taller than wide. Multiple posters visible from single viewpoint.
image_recognition.historic_note: Invented by Berlin printer Ernst Litfaß in 1855. Now managed primarily by Ströer and Wall (JCDecaux subsidiary) in Germany.

dimensions.note: No single standardized size. AWK minimum roughly 8.56 × 3.56 m. Common sizes: 14×4 m, 18×5 m, custom up to 40+ m wide.

visual_characteristics: Extremely large landscape format — building-scale or larger
Often covers entire building gable (Brandmauer) or construction hoarding
Visible seams from individual printed panels joined together
External spotlight bars often mounted on extended arms away from surface
At highway scale: readable from 200–500m viewing distance
When on building wall: artwork scaled to architectural features, may incorporate windows/edges
Print quality visible as pixelation/dot pattern at very close range
image_recognition.common_context: Building gables, construction sites, highway roadsides, stadiums, airports
image_recognition.size_context: Multiple times larger than a bus. Can cover entire multi-story building face.

dimensions.note: OMC 6-Sheet standard. International standard bus shelter format. Also called '6-sheet' (6 standard sheet sizes). German equivalent: CLP (119×175 cm).

visual_characteristics: Portrait orientation, 2:3 ratio (same as A-series paper but at 1.2×1.8m scale)
Integrated into glass bus shelter side panel or back panel
Uniform backlit glow — same appearance as CLP
Often within 2–3 meters of road or bus stop signage
Passengers seated/standing immediately adjacent for scale reference
Printed on translucent vinyl — backlight shows through from rear
Frame integrated into shelter architecture, minimal border visibility
image_recognition.common_context: Bus stops, tram stops, metro station entrances — inside or adjacent to shelter structure
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Contextually always near a bus stop or shelter. Nearly identical appearance to CLP. Slightly wider proportioned than CLP.

dimensions.note: OMC standard. Most common UK roadside billboard. US equivalent: 14×48 ft 'Bulletin'.

visual_characteristics: Landscape format, 2:1 ratio (twice as wide as tall)
Large freestanding structure on single or twin metal posts
Poster surface approx. 6m wide × 3m high — twice the width of a large SUV
Visible paper poster sheets (typically 12 strips × 4 rows = 48 sheets historically)
White border on all sides within frame
Steel cabinet frame, typically back-painted black or dark gray
Often at roadside at 4–8 meters elevation
If internally illuminated: backlit variant glows uniformly
image_recognition.common_context: UK/Irish roadside, major junctions, retail parks
image_recognition.size_context: Approximately the footprint of a shipping container (6m × 3m)

dimensions.note: OMC standard. Double the width of 48-sheet.

visual_characteristics: Extreme landscape ratio (4:1) — dramatically wider than tall
Two 48-sheet billboard frames combined side-by-side
Approximately 12m wide — length of a double-decker bus
Dominant visual presence on roadsides and motorways
Often on large gantry structures spanning multiple lanes

dimensions.note: Same physical frame dimensions as CLP. Screen replaces static poster.

screen_specs.brightness_note: DarkSky: digital units can be 10× brighter than traditional backlit formats (res-012)

energy.note: Actual consumption depends on brightness setting and operation hours. 24/7 operation typical.

visual_characteristics: Same frame and dimensions as analog CLP — only the face differs
LED pixel grid visible at close range (0.5–2m): fine dot matrix pattern
Dynamic content: image changes every 10–15 seconds
Extremely high brightness compared to surrounding environment — visible in direct sunlight
No visible paper/vinyl texture — smooth glass or acrylic protective layer
Colors are highly saturated and luminous, especially reds and yellows
At night: disproportionately bright compared to street lighting
Visible scan lines or refresh artifacts in video/fast capture (phone camera)
Bezels slightly wider than analog CLP to accommodate electronics housing
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_analog: Pixel structure visible up close. Dynamic content (changes). Much higher brightness. No paper seams or texture. No light diffusion glow at edges (unlike backlit).
image_recognition.privacy_note: Many DCLP units contain cameras for audience measurement (see priv-005, priv-006). Camera aperture typically visible as small dark circle in corner or center of frame.

visual_characteristics: Portrait orientation in bus shelter or freestanding unit
Same contextual placement as analog 6-sheet (bus stops)
High-brightness LED screen — visible in all weather and daylight conditions
Often rotates between multiple advertisers
Camera sensor sometimes integrated at top of screen bezel (audience measurement)

dimensions.note: No single standard. Highway format typically 14m × 4m or similar. Urban format 3–8m wide.

screen_specs.pixel_pitch_mm: 6–16 (highway: coarser pitch for long viewing distance)
screen_specs.brightness_note: Outdoor highway-grade LED. Up to 10× brighter than illuminated static billboard (res-012).

energy.typical_range: 29000–94000 kWh/year depending on size and brightness
energy.typical_range_source: OAAA Digital Billboard Energy Report (Louis Berger Group)

visual_characteristics: Landscape orientation, typically 3:1 to 4:1 aspect ratio
LED pixel grid clearly visible from distances under 10m
Extreme brightness — washes out surrounding environment in photographs
Full-motion video capability (distinguishable from still image by motion blur in photos)
Cabinet structure behind screen visible from sides: ventilation fins, power housing
Often elevated on single or twin steel poles 4–12m above ground
Visible pixel clusters / 'dead pixels' occasionally on aging units
At night: significant light spill — illuminates ground and surrounding structures below

highway_variant.coarser_pixel_pitch: P10–P16 — pixel grid less visible at distance
highway_variant.safety_note: US DOT-funded study reports correlation with higher crash rates in highway DOOH influence zones; use site-specific analysis rather than a universal percentage (safety-007)

dimensions.note: No standard dimensions. Custom installations from 10m² to 500m+². Often wraps building corners, curves, or spans multiple structures.

screen_specs.note: Highest-grade outdoor LED. Premium locations command premium brightness specs.

visual_characteristics: Non-rectangular or unusually large form factor
Often wraps building corners (L-shape or curved LED panels)
May include 3D elements or content that appears to protrude from surface
Landmark locations: iconic intersections, Times Square-style environments
Extremely high ambient light contribution to surrounding street
Often 4K+ resolution content — visible production quality

dimensions.note: Digital replacement for Litfaßsäule. Some modern variants are fully LED-wrapped.

visual_characteristics: Cylindrical form factor — same shape as traditional Litfaßsäule
LED screens visible as rectangular bright panels on column surface
Content changes dynamically — multiple different ads visible on different faces simultaneously
Much brighter than traditional paper column — visible from distance at night
Camera sensors may be visible between screen panels

subtypes.name: Bus full wrap
subtypes.note: Printed vinyl wrap on bus exterior. Not a screen.

subtypes.name: Bus shelter digital screen
subtypes.dimensions: same as DCLP or D6 (see above)

visual_characteristics: Bus exterior vinyl wraps: matte printed surface follows curve of vehicle body
Platform digital screens: multiple horizontal screens at eye-height on platform
Interior screens: ceiling or seat-back mounted, lower brightness
Dynamic content rotation, often synchronized across platform screens

dimensions.note: Custom dimensions, typically covering one or more building facades. Often 20–60m wide, 10–40m high. Net/mesh material allows wind permeability.

visual_characteristics: Massive scale — often covers entire building or construction scaffold
Mesh/net texture visible up close — print has slight transparency
Visible rope, cable, or net attachment points at regular intervals
At construction sites: combined with safety netting (green/orange visible through mesh)
Single unified large-scale print — often portrait orientation on tall buildings
Surface slightly billows or ripples in wind
At night: external spotlights cast uneven illumination from below or sides
image_recognition.common_context: Building renovations, construction sites, major urban development areas

dimensions.note: AWK 8/1 format. Two CLP-sized panels stacked vertically on a single post unit. Total visible area 118.5 × 350 cm. Ströer designation: '8/1 Säule'.

visual_characteristics: Very tall narrow portrait format — roughly 3× taller than wide (approx. 1:3 ratio)
Approximately 3.5m high — towers above eye level significantly
Same backlit glow as CLP but much taller structure
Single post/column mounting — thin profile visible from the side
Often displays two different adverts stacked (top half / bottom half)
Or single artwork spanning full 118.5 × 350 cm
Size reference: roughly as tall as a double-decker bus is wide
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Taller than CLP (175cm) but same width. Unlike Litfaßsäule, it is flat-faced (not cylindrical). Unlike digital CLS, no pixel structure.

dimensions.note: AWK 18/1 format. Same dimensions as Großfläche but with internal backlight illumination. WallDecaux/Ströer premium format. Also called 'Megalight' or '9m²-Board' in some markets.

visual_characteristics: Same dimensions as Großfläche (356×252 cm) but with uniform internal backlight glow
Landscape orientation, wider than tall (approx. 1.41:1 ratio)
Even luminous illumination from behind — no external spotlight fixtures
White/neutral glow visible at panel edges, especially in low light
Premium locations: high-traffic intersections, shopping streets, transit areas
Aluminum frame, typically slim profile
Much brighter than externally illuminated Großfläche — visible from greater distance
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Same size as Großfläche but internally backlit (uniform glow, no spotlight fixtures). Different from DCLB which has LED pixel structure. Different from Großfläche beleuchtet which has external lamps attached.

dimensions.note: Same physical frame as CLB. WallDecaux Berlin/Hamburg flagship digital format. Full HD (1920×1080) or 4K (3840×2160).

screen_specs.note: WallDecaux Berlin contract: 330 DCLB units (9m² digital panels) across the city.

visual_characteristics: Same large landscape frame as CLB/Großfläche — 3.56m × 2.52m
LED pixel grid visible at close range (under 5m)
High-brightness screen — significantly brighter than surrounding backlit panels
Dynamic content: changes every 10 seconds
Often has slightly deeper cabinet than analog CLB (electronics housing)
Premium city center and transport hub locations
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_clb: Pixel structure visible up close. Dynamic changing content. Brighter than analog backlit. No paper/vinyl surface.

dimensions.note: Ströer Public Video Station format. DB Fernverkehr Infoscreens (S-Bahn/U-Bahn/Fernbahnhof). Resolution varies by network and generation. Newer units: Full HD 1920×1080.

visual_characteristics: Medium-sized landscape screen (46–65 inch typical) in transit environment
Often ceiling-suspended in groups of 2–4 screens in train station concourse
LCD or LED display — uniform rectangular face, no pixel texture visible at normal viewing
Content mix: advertising + real-time departure/arrival information
Often in branded housing (Ströer black frame, DB corporate design)
Lower brightness than outdoor screens — adapted for indoor lighting conditions
Visible content rotation between information and advertising every few seconds
image_recognition.common_context: Train station platforms, concourses, U-Bahn entrances, shopping center food courts
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Always indoors or in covered transit environment. Often displays travel information alongside ads. Part of coordinated screen networks.

dimensions.note: No standardized dimensions. Freestanding tall narrow digital display on pedestal. Airport, premium retail, city center locations. Some double-sided.

visual_characteristics: Tall narrow freestanding structure — portrait screen on a pedestal base
Typically 2.5–4m total height, base is solid cabinet housing electronics
Slim profile — much narrower than tall (approx. 1:3 to 1:5 ratio)
Often double-sided: two screens back-to-back on same pedestal
Premium finishes: brushed aluminum, black glass base housing
High-brightness portrait screen — vivid colors in well-lit environments
Sometimes interactive/touchscreen with information kiosk function
Often positioned at entrances, concourse centers, key pedestrian junctions
image_recognition.common_context: Airport terminal gates, premium mall atriums, hotel lobbies, convention centers
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_similar: Unlike CLS, it is always digital and on a pedestal base (not a post). Unlike digital CLP, it is much taller and narrower with a substantial base unit.

formats.name_en: Airport Large Format / Digital Deluxe Board

dimensions.note: No single standard. Each major airport has bespoke large-format installations. Frankfurt example: 43m² ultra-wide LED wall.

screen_specs.note: Frankfurt Airport: ~500 digital media displays, 50%+ of advertising inventory is digital. First 3D DOOH in Germany at FRA.

visual_characteristics: Ultra-wide landscape LED wall — dramatically wider than tall (3:1 to 6:1 or wider)
Very high ambient brightness to compete with large terminal windows/skylights
Often spans full wall of terminal corridor or departure hall
Premium content production quality — large budget campaigns
Sometimes 3D/anamorphic content (objects appearing to float off screen)
Multiple screens tiled seamlessly to create single large image surface
LED seams visible at close range but invisible from passenger viewing distance
image_recognition.common_context: Airport departure/arrival halls, terminal corridors, gate areas
image_recognition.captive_audience_note: Airport formats target captive, dwell-time-rich audience — average 2–3 hour dwell time before flights

dimensions.note: Standard billboard frame size (e.g., Großfläche 356×252 cm). Mechanical prism strips rotate to cycle 3 different ads.

formats.note: Not electronic/LED — mechanical rotation of triangular prism strips or full banner scroll. Pre-digital era dynamic format.

visual_characteristics: Same frame as standard billboard
Horizontal prism strips (Tri-Vision): visible vertical lines across image face from angle
During rotation: image appears to disintegrate into strips before resolving to next ad
In motion: visible mechanical movement — strips rotate simultaneously
Static appearance between rotations identical to paper billboard
Motor housing visible at edges or base of frame
image_recognition.distinguishing_from_digital: Visible mechanical strips. Not self-illuminated. Lower brightness than LED. No pixel structure.

comparison_table.description: Quick reference for AI agents to compare formats at a glance
comparison_table.note: Energy figures from res-001 where documented. null = not documented in dataset.
